Summary: APOE4 is the single greatest genetic risk factor for sporadic Alzheimer’s disease and was studied in CRISPR/Cas9-edited human iPSC-derived neurons, astrocytes, and microglia-like cells. Transcriptional profiling identified hundreds of differentially expressed genes per cell type and implicated synaptic function in neurons, lipid metabolism in astrocytes, and immune response in microglia-like cells. APOE4 neurons had increased synapse number and elevated Aβ42 secretion versus isogenic APOE3 cells. APOE4 astrocytes showed impaired Aβ uptake and cholesterol accumulation. APOE4 microglia-like cells exhibited altered morphologies that correlated with reduced Aβ phagocytosis. Converting APOE4 to APOE3 in isogenic and sporadic AD patient–derived iPSCs reversed or attenuated most AD-related phenotypes across neurons, glia, and organoids. However, conversion did not reduce Aβ42 secretion from sAD neurons and the reduction in early endosome numbers was not statistically significant. Additional factors in that sAD iPSC-derived cell line likely confound the observed AD-related pathologies.
References: Lin, Y. T., Seo, J., Gao, F., Feldman, H. M., Wen, H. L., Penney, J., Cam, H. P., Gjoneska, E., Raja, W. K., Cheng, J., Rueda, R., Kritskiy, O., Abdurrob, F., Peng, Z., Milo, B., Yu, C. J., Elmsaouri, S., Dey, D., Ko, T., … Tsai, L. H. (2018). APOE4 Causes Widespread Molecular and Cellular Alterations Associated with Alzheimer's Disease Phenotypes in Human iPSC-Derived Brain Cell Types. Neuron, 98(6), 1141-1154.e7. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuron.2018.05.008
